behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA applies: this behavior depends on real OS filesystem watcher recursion behavior for nested paths. LOWER-LEVEL-MISS-PROOF: an Integration test with mocked watcher events can pass even if the actual watcher backend never emits events for subdirectories (common misconfiguration/OS-specific gap). REJECTION-GATE: kept System because behavior text explicitly targets real watched-directory event coverage ("placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed"); no AI/LLM dependence, so Agentic/Workflow are disqualified.
behaviors.test_description: Start the daemon against a real temporary directory tree, create a new file under a nested subdirectory, and assert exactly one transformed output appears in the output directory for that nested file path.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS points to module handoff: dedup correctness is about interaction between event intake + dedup state + pipeline dispatch, but does not require real OS/network/subprocess. INTEGRATION-BRAKE check: (a) no real OS primitive required for the core rule, (b) no AI output dependency, (c) no multi-agentic chain. LOWER-LEVEL-MISS-PROOF: a Unit test of only the dedup function can miss a real failure where the caller normalizes paths differently between first and second request, causing duplicate processing despite correct local dedup logic. REJECTION-GATE: considered System but rejected because behavior text is about dedup time-window semantics, not OS-specific event mechanics.
behaviors.test_description: Wire real in-process modules (queue/event handler, dedup store, dispatcher) with a controllable clock; submit two requests for the same canonical path within the window and assert only one dispatch occurs, then verify dispatch resumes after window expiry.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA and MOCK-HIDE apply directly: duplicate-event bursts are OS/watcher-backend behavior. LOWER-LEVEL-MISS-PROOF: an Integration test that injects a single synthesized event (or idealized duplicates) can miss real timing/order patterns from the OS that trigger double-processing. REJECTION-GATE: behavior text explicitly says "operating system emits multiple filesystem events," which disqualifies lower-only in-process testing; no AI/LLM dependence, so Agentic/Workflow are disqualified.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon with a real watched directory and perform file writes known to trigger multiple low-level events; assert the pipeline executes once and exactly one output artifact is produced per logical file write.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA applies: directory deletion/recreation watcher reattachment is OS-level behavior and frequently backend-specific. LOWER-LEVEL-MISS-PROOF: an Integration test with mocked watcher lifecycle can pass while real watcher subscriptions are lost after inode replacement, so new files are never observed until restart. REJECTION-GATE: behavior text explicitly requires runtime handling of actual directory removal/recreation, which disqualifies purely in-process fakes; no AI/LLM dependence, so Agentic/Workflow are disqualified.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon on a real temp watch path, remove the watched directory, recreate it at the same path, then add a new file and verify it is processed without restarting the daemon.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA applies because correctness depends on real filesystem timestamp semantics/resolution and event timing under concurrent arrivals. LOWER-LEVEL-MISS-PROOF: an Integration test with injected timestamps can miss failures caused by real FS timestamp granularity/ties or stat-field differences that reorder files incorrectly in production. REJECTION-GATE: considered Integration but rejected because behavior text ties ordering to actual "creation timestamps" under simultaneous arrival, a real filesystem property; no AI/LLM dependence, so Agentic/Workflow are disqualified.
behaviors.test_description: Create multiple files nearly simultaneously in the real watched directory, record their actual creation timestamps from filesystem metadata, and assert daemon processing order matches the expected timestamp ordering policy (including tie handling).

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA and MOCK-HIDE apply: partial-write visibility and event timing are OS/filesystem behaviors; in-process fakes often present atomically-complete files and hide this failure. LOWER-LEVEL-MISS-PROOF: an Integration test with mocked file reads may never expose truncated/intermediate content races, while real slow writes can trigger premature processing. REJECTION-GATE: behavior text explicitly depends on real-time write completion state ("still being written"), which disqualifies lower in-process-only testing; no AI/LLM dependence, so Agentic/Workflow are disqualified.
behaviors.test_description: Write a large file in chunks with delays into the real watched directory, trigger arrival during the write, and assert processing starts only after final chunk/close and output reflects complete file content.
